message:
feat(plan): Add dynamic lab equivalence oracle and algebraic law gating

bd-1lka: Implement execute_plan_in_lab() for generic PlanDag execution in
the lab runtime, plus run_lab_dynamic_equivalence() oracle that verifies
original vs optimized plans match across multiple seeds. Includes
LabDynamicEquivalenceReport with all_ok() and failure_summary(), spawn
helpers for all node types, and 8 comprehensive tests.

bd-2m0t: Add AlgebraicLaw enum (Associativity, Commutativity,
Distributivity, TimeoutSimplification) with required_laws() on
RewriteRule and permits()/allows_law() on RewritePolicy. Gate TimeoutMin
under timeout_simplification flag. Update conservative() to enable
restricted distributivity. Add 9 gating tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1193","title":"Remote spawn: task execution on remote nodes","description":"# Remote Spawn Implementation

## Goal

Enable spawning tasks on remote nodes while preserving region ownership and structured concurrency guarantees.

## Background

Remote spawn extends local spawn semantics:
- Local: cx.spawn(task) → TaskHandle
- Remote: cx.remote_spawn(node, task) → RemoteHandle

The RemoteHandle provides the same interface but operations go over the network.

## Core Types

### RemoteHandle<T>
```rust
pub struct RemoteHandle<T> {
    /// Unique ID for this remote task
    task_id: RemoteTaskId,
    
    /// Node where task is running
    node: NodeId,
    
    /// Lease for the remote resource
    lease: Lease,
    
    /// Channel for result (when task completes)
    result: oneshot::Receiver<Outcome<T, Error>>,
}

impl<T> RemoteHandle<T> {
    /// Wait for task completion
    async fn join(self) -> Outcome<T, Error>;
    
    /// Cancel the remote task
    async fn cancel(&self, reason: CancelReason);
    
    /// Check if task is still running
    async fn is_alive(&self) -> bool;
    
    /// Renew the lease
    async fn renew_lease(&mut self, duration: Duration) -> Result<(), LeaseError>;
}
```

### RemoteTaskId
```rust
pub struct RemoteTaskId {
    /// Originating node
    origin: NodeId,
    
    /// Local task ID on originating node
    local_id: TaskId,
    
    /// Unique nonce for this spawn
    nonce: u64,
}
```

### NodeId
```rust
pub struct NodeId {
    /// Unique node identifier
    id: u64,
    
    /// Network address (for connection)
    addr: SocketAddr,
    
    /// Generation (for detecting node restarts)
    generation: u32,
}
```

## Wire Protocol

### SpawnRequest
```rust
struct SpawnRequest {
    task_id: RemoteTaskId,
    task_bytes: Vec<u8>,  // Serialized task
    parent_region: RegionId,
    budget: Budget,
    lease_duration: Duration,
}
```

### SpawnResponse
```rust
enum SpawnResponse {
    Accepted { lease: Lease },
    Rejected { reason: SpawnRejection },
}
```

## Serialization

Tasks must be serializable to send over the network:
```rust
pub trait RemoteTask: Task + Serialize + DeserializeOwned {
    type Output: Serialize + DeserializeOwned;
}
```

Use serde with bincode for efficient binary serialization.

## Region Ownership

Remote tasks are owned by the local region that spawned them:
- Region close cancels remote tasks
- Remote task failure can fail-fast local region
- Quiescence requires remote tasks complete

## Implementation Notes

- Build on existing RemoteSpawn scaffold in src/remote/
- Use TCP for reliable delivery (HTTP/2 or custom protocol)
- Integrate with IdempotencyRegistry for retry safety
- LeaseManager handles lease renewal background task

## Error Handling

- NetworkError: Connection failed
- NodeUnavailable: Target node not reachable
- TaskRejected: Node refused spawn (resource limits)
- LeaseExpired: Lease not renewed in time

## Testing

- Local simulation with multiple LabRuntime instances
- Network failure injection
- Lease expiry scenarios
- Cross-node cancellation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] RemoteHandle with join/cancel/renew
- [ ] RemoteTaskId with origin tracking
- [ ] NodeId with generation counter
- [ ] SpawnRequest/Response wire types
- [ ] Serialization trait for remote tasks
- [ ] Region ownership of remote tasks
- [ ] Integration with LeaseManager
- [ ] Basic network transport","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:21:31.178354146Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:43:05.412897403Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:43:05.412798759Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed","phase4","remote-spawn"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1193","depends_on_id":"bd-1jis","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:04Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1193","depends_on_id":"bd-2c2i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:04Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1193","depends_on_id":"bd-3gmj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:04Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1193","depends_on_id":"bd-3sst","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:04Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-12rg","title":"[ALIEN][SCHED] Lyapunov-Guided Scheduler Integration (Cancel Convergence)","description":"## Goal

Integrate the existing Lyapunov governor (potential-function scheduling) into the *real* runtime scheduler so cancellation/drain/quiescence converges quickly and predictably.

Asupersync already treats cancellation as a protocol. The governor provides a principled way to prioritize work that reduces "cancel debt" and outstanding obligations.

## Potential function sketch

Define a non-negative potential V(Σ) over runtime state Σ, e.g.:

V(Σ) = w1 * (# unresolved obligations)
     + w2 * (deadline slack penalties)
     + w3 * (# pending cancel requests)
     + w4 * (IO in-flight debt)
     + ...

A scheduling step should (heuristically) maximize expected drift reduction ΔV subject to correctness constraints.

## Constraints

- Deterministic: decisions must be deterministic given Σ.
- Preserve safety invariants: lane ordering (cancel > timed > ready) should remain correct, or any relaxation must be proven safe and tested.
- No overhead when disabled: default config keeps current scheduler behavior.

## Deliverables

- A scheduler policy seam that can consult the governor.
- Efficient state snapshots (avoid allocations on hot path).
- Deterministic tie-break rules.
- Debug-only "galaxy-brain" ledger trace events for: V breakdown, candidate comparison, chosen action.
- Tests: monotone convergence on synthetic scenarios + no regressions in quiescence/oracles.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:09:11.308842108Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T00:09:11.309470937Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","cancellation","obligation","scheduler"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-12rg","depends_on_id":"bd-2zce","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:13:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}

## Purpose
Comprehensive verification for async streams (x3p, tokio-stream equivalent) ensuring combinator correctness and cancel-safety.

## Test Categories

### 1. Unit Tests
- Stream trait: poll_next
- StreamExt: map, filter, filter_map, take, skip
- StreamExt: chain, zip, merge, select
- StreamExt: fold, collect, for_each
- Channel streams: mpsc, broadcast, watch
- Interval stream: tick, tick_with

### 2. E2E Scenarios
| Scenario | Verifies |
|----------|----------|
| Basic iteration | Stream consumption |
| Map + filter chain | Combinator composition |
| Merge streams | Interleaved output |
| Backpressure | Slow consumer handling |
| Cancel mid-stream | Clean termination |
| Infinite stream + take | Bounded consumption |
| Error propagation | Stream error handling |

### 3. Cancel-Safety Tests
- Cancel during next(): no item loss
- Cancel during collect(): partial results
- Cancel merged stream: all sources cancelled

### 4. Memory Tests
- No buffering leaks
- Bounded memory for infinite streams
- Drop semantics for pending items

## Logging Requirements
- Stream events logged with stream IDs
- On failure: dump pending items, combinator state
- Item flow metrics: produced, consumed, dropped

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All combinators have unit tests
- [ ] E2E scenarios pass under lab runtime
- [ ] Cancel-safety verified for all operations
- [ ] Memory bounded for all scenarios
- [ ] `cargo test stream` passes deterministically

## Verification Commands
```bash
cargo test --package asupersync --lib stream
cargo test --test stream_e2e

 {"id":"bd-1sf","title":"Implement ObligationState and ObligationKind enums","description":"# ObligationState and ObligationKind Enums

## Purpose
These types define the two-phase effect system's tracking of linear resources. Obligations are tokens that MUST be resolved (committed or aborted) before their owning region closes.

## ObligationKind
```rust
enum ObligationKind {
    // Channel send permit (reserve capacity, then send)
    SendPermit,
    
    // Message acknowledgment (receive, process, then ack/nack)
    Ack,
    
    // Time-bounded resource lease (must renew or expire)
    Lease,
    
    // In-flight I/O operation (must complete or cancel)
    IoOp,
}
```

## ObligationState
```rust
enum ObligationState {
    // Obligation created, waiting for resolution
    Reserved,
    
    // Obligation fulfilled (effect took place)
    Committed,
    
    // Obligation cleanly cancelled (no effect)
    Aborted,
    
    // ERROR: Obligation lost (holder completed without resolving)
    Leaked,
}
```

## State Transitions

```
Reserved ──────► Committed
    │
    ├──────────► Aborted
    │
    └──────────► Leaked (error state, detected by runtime)
```

All three terminal states (Committed, Aborted, Leaked) are absorbing - once reached, no further transitions.

## Why These Obligation Kinds?

### SendPermit
Two-phase channel send prevents message loss under cancellation:
```rust
let permit = tx.reserve(cx).await?;  // Reserve capacity (cancel-safe)
permit.send(message);                 // Commit: actually send
// OR drop permit → Aborted (capacity released, no message lost)
```

### Ack
Two-phase receive with acknowledgment for reliable messaging:
```rust
let (item, ack) = rx.recv_with_ack(cx).await?;  // Receive but don't dequeue
process(item);
ack.commit();  // Dequeue permanently
// OR drop ack → Aborted (nack, message redelivered)
```

### Lease
Time-bounded resource ownership:
```rust
let lease = resource.lease(duration, cx).await?;
// Use resource...
lease.renew(duration);  // Extend lease
// OR lease expires → Aborted (resource released)
```

### IoOp
In-flight I/O operations bound to region:
```rust
let op = io.submit_read(buffer, cx).await?;  // Submit (region can't close yet)
let result = op.complete().await;             // Wait for completion
// OR op.cancel() → Aborted (I/O cancelled)
```

## ObligationState Semantics

### Reserved
- Obligation exists and must be resolved
- Blocks region close (quiescence requires zero reserved obligations)
- Shows up in obligation registry under holder's region

### Committed
- The effect took place successfully
- E.g., message was sent, ack was committed, I/O completed
- This is the "happy path" resolution

### Aborted
- The effect was cleanly cancelled
- No data loss, no side effects
- Capacity/resources released back
- This is the "safe cancellation" path

### Leaked
- ERROR STATE: Holder completed without resolving
- This indicates a bug in user code or library
- In lab mode: panic immediately
- In prod mode: log error, attempt recovery, continue
- Triggers futurelock detection

## Linear Resource Discipline

Obligations are LINEAR resources in the logic sense:
```
reserve : Cx → Obligation<K, 1>      // Introduce obligation
commit  : Obligation<K, 1> → Cx → () // Eliminate obligation
abort   : Obligation<K, 1> → Cx → () // Eliminate obligation
```

The `1` annotation means "exactly one use." Dropping without explicit resolution is detected and triggers Leaked.

## VASS/Petri Net View

For verification, obligations can be modeled as a vector addition system:
```
marking(r, k) = count of Reserved obligations of kind k in region r

reserve(k) → marking += 1
commit/abort(k) → marking -= 1
region_close requires marking = 0 for all kinds
```

## Implementation Requirements

1. **Both enums must be Copy, Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq**
2. **ObligationKind should have Display** for tracing
3. **ObligationState::is_terminal()**: Returns true for Committed/Aborted/Leaked
4. **ObligationState::is_resolved()**: Returns true for Committed/Aborted (not Leaked)
5. **ObligationState::is_error()**: Returns true only for Leaked

## Drop Semantics

When an obligation token is dropped:
- If Reserved → transition to Aborted AND emit trace event
- If already terminal → no-op

In lab mode, dropping a Reserved obligation can optionally panic (configurable).

## Testing Requirements

1. All terminal states are absorbing
2. Leaked is only reachable from Reserved
3. Committed/Aborted are mutually exclusive endpoints
4. Obligation tracking correctly counts per-region

## Invariant Support

### INV-OBLIGATION-BOUNDED
```rust
∀o: O[o].state = Reserved ⟹
    T[O[o].holder].state ∈ {Running, CancelRequested, Cancelling, Finalizing}
```

### INV-OBLIGATION-LINEAR
```rust
∀o: O[o].state ∈ {Committed, Aborted, Leaked} is absorbing
```

### I2: Region close = quiescence
```rust
R[r].state = Closed(_) requires
    ∀o where O[o].region = r: O[o].state ≠ Reserved
```

## Example Usage

```rust
// Two-phase send
let permit: SendPermit<T> = tx.reserve(cx).await?;
// permit.obligation_state() == Reserved

if should_send {
    permit.send(value);  // → Committed
} else {
    drop(permit);  // → Aborted
}

// If cx is cancelled and permit dropped without explicit resolution:
// → Aborted (safe default)
```

## References
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §1.7 (Obligation States)
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §3.4 (Obligations transitions)
- asupersync_plan_v4.md §8 (Two-phase effects + linear obligations)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Defines `ObligationKind` and `ObligationState` per the spec (Reserved/Committed/Aborted/Leaked).
- States are absorbing after resolution (Committed/Aborted/Leaked).
- Implements deterministic, trace-friendly formatting.
- Unit tests cover state ordering (if any), absorbing behavior, and conversions used by the registry.

 {"id":"bd-293f","title":"Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements","description":"# Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements

## Overview

Beyond critical bugs, the scheduler has several performance and fairness 
issues that impact throughput, latency, and predictability under load.

## Background & Context

The scheduler's performance characteristics directly impact:
- **Throughput**: Tasks processed per second
- **Latency**: Time from task ready to execution
- **Fairness**: Equal treatment of equal-priority tasks
- **Efficiency**: CPU cycles per task

## Performance Issues

1. **PriorityScheduler O(n) insert** (`priority.rs:27-43`)
   - Linear scan for insertion position
   - Degrades with large queues

2. **Imprecise wakeup strategy** (`three_lane.rs:127-134`)
   - Always wakes first parker
   - No load balancing consideration

3. **Try-lock contention in stealing** (`three_lane.rs:367`)
   - Multiple stealers contend on same lock
   - Wasted CPU cycles

## Fairness Issues

4. **RNG tie-breaking bias** (`priority.rs:27-70`)
   - Single RNG hint used for all decisions
   - Predictable, non-random selection

5. **Duplicate scheduling across queues** (`priority.rs:131-135`)
   - Task can be in both local and global queue
   - Potential double execution

6. **Only stealing from ready lane** (`three_lane.rs:354-387`)
   - Cancel/timed work not stolen
   - Uneven work distribution

## Performance Goals

- O(1) or O(log n) operations on hot path
- Minimal lock contention
- Efficient work distribution
- Predictable latency

- Benchmark suite for scheduler operations

 {"id":"bd-2aiq","title":"Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients - NATS/Redis/Kafka Integration","description":"# Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients

## Overview
Implements first-party clients for major message queue systems (NATS, Redis Pub/Sub,
Kafka) with full Cx integration, structured concurrency, and cancel-correct semantics.

## Background & Motivation
Anyone using Tokio will say 'I can use async-nats, rdkafka, redis-rs for message queues.'
We need first-party, cancel-correct alternatives that integrate seamlessly with
Asupersync's structured concurrency model.

## Key Differentiators from Tokio Ecosystem

### Cancel-Correct Consumption
In Tokio, if a task consuming messages is cancelled, messages may be lost.
In Asupersync:
- Message delivery is an obligation
- Consumption must be ack/nack/committed/aborted
- Cancellation drains in-flight handlers gracefully
- No 'message lost because task dropped'

### Backpressure as First-Class Protocol
- Explicit credit windows
- Bounded queues with budget integration
- Producer slowdown when consumers lag

### Structured Lifecycle
- Connection owned by region
- Region close gracefully disconnects
- Child tasks for message handlers scoped to region

## Message Queue Clients

### 1. NATS Client
- Pure Rust implementation (no C dependencies)
- Subject-based pub/sub
- Request-reply pattern
- JetStream support (durable streams)

### 2. Redis Client
- Redis protocol (RESP)
- GET/SET/HGET/etc. operations
- Pub/Sub subscriptions
- Streams (XREAD, XADD, XACK)

### 3. Kafka Client
- Producer with exactly-once semantics
- Consumer groups with commit offsets
- Transactional support
- (May wrap librdkafka for protocol complexity)

## Integration Points
- All clients use Asupersync TCP/TLS stack
- Cx checkpoints for cancellation
- Budget integration for rate limiting
- Tracing for observability

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. NATS client core (connect, pub/sub)
2. NATS JetStream integration
3. Redis client core (commands, connection)
4. Redis Pub/Sub and Streams
5. Kafka producer
6. Kafka consumer
7. Message queue E2E tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] NATS client with pub/sub and request-reply
- [ ] Redis client with commands and pub/sub
- [ ] Kafka producer/consumer with commit semantics
- [ ] All clients cancel-correct
- [ ] Backpressure handling
- [ ] E2E tests with real services (Docker)
- [ ] Performance comparable to Tokio alternatives","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:23:22.858029369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.021181164Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.021075958Z","close_reason":"All children completed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","messaging"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2aiq","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2sad","title":"HTTP/2 PRIORITY: validate stream cannot depend on itself","description":"# Bug: Stream Self-Dependency Not Validated in PRIORITY Frame

## Location
`src/http/h2/frame.rs:445-469`

## Issue Description

The PRIORITY frame parser extracts dependency information but never validates 
that a stream cannot depend on itself:

```rust
let exclusive = payload[0] & 0x80 != 0;
let dependency = ((u32::from(payload[0]) & 0x7f) << 24)
    | (u32::from(payload[1]) << 16)
    | (u32::from(payload[2]) << 8)
    | u32::from(payload[3]);
let weight = payload[4].saturating_add(1);
```

## RFC Requirement

RFC 7540 Section 5.3.1 states:
> A stream cannot depend on itself. An endpoint MUST treat this as a 
> stream error (Section 5.4.2) of type PROTOCOL_ERROR.

## Impact

- **Severity**: LOW (protocol violation, not security)
- **Exploitability**: N/A (no direct exploit, just non-compliance)
- **Impact**: Potential dependency tree corruption, conformance failure

## Proposed Fix

Add validation in frame parsing:

```rust
// In PriorityFrame::parse()
let dependency = ...;

if dependency == header.stream_id {
    return Err(H2Error::stream_error(
        header.stream_id,
        ErrorCode::ProtocolError,
        "stream cannot depend on itself",
    ));
}
```

---

## Comprehensive Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (tests/h2/priority_frame_tests.rs)

#### Self-Dependency Detection Tests
1. **test_priority_self_dependency_stream_1**: Stream 1 depends on 1 = stream error
2. **test_priority_self_dependency_stream_3**: Stream 3 depends on 3 = stream error
3. **test_priority_self_dependency_max_stream**: Stream 2^31-1 depends on self = error
4. **test_priority_self_dependency_error_code**: Error must be PROTOCOL_ERROR
5. **test_priority_self_dependency_stream_error**: Must be stream error, not connection error

#### Valid Dependency Tests
6. **test_priority_valid_dependency**: Stream 3 depends on 1 = success
7. **test_priority_root_dependency**: Stream depends on 0 (root) = success
8. **test_priority_reverse_dependency**: Higher stream depends on lower = success
9. **test_priority_forward_dependency**: Lower stream depends on higher = success

#### Exclusive Flag Tests
10. **test_priority_exclusive_with_self_dependency**: Exclusive + self-dep = error
11. **test_priority_exclusive_valid**: Exclusive on valid dependency = success
12. **test_priority_exclusive_root**: Exclusive dependency on root = success

#### Weight Tests
13. **test_priority_weight_min**: Weight 0 in payload = weight 1
14. **test_priority_weight_max**: Weight 255 in payload = weight 256
15. **test_priority_weight_mid**: Weight 127 in payload = weight 128

#### Frame Structure Tests
16. **test_priority_wrong_length**: Payload != 5 bytes = error
17. **test_priority_stream_0**: PRIORITY on stream 0 = connection error
18. **test_priority_headers_priority**: HEADERS with PRIORITY flag includes priority fields

### Integration Tests (tests/h2/priority_integration.rs)

```rust
#[test]
fn integration_priority_self_dep_rejected_by_server() {
    let (mut client, mut server) = create_connected_pair();
    
    // Client sends PRIORITY with self-dependency
    client.send_priority(/*stream=*/ 1, /*depends_on=*/ 1, /*weight=*/ 16, /*exclusive=*/ false);
    
    // Server should send RST_STREAM with PROTOCOL_ERROR
    let frame = server.receive_frame();
    if let Some(Frame::RstStream(rst)) = frame {
        assert_eq!(rst.stream_id, 1);
        assert_eq!(rst.error_code, ErrorCode::ProtocolError);
    } else {
        panic!("expected RST_STREAM, got {:?}", frame);
    }
}

#[test]
fn integration_priority_self_dep_via_headers() {
    let (mut client, mut server) = create_connected_pair();
    
    // HEADERS frame with PRIORITY flag where stream depends on itself
    client.send_headers_with_priority(
        /*stream=*/ 3,
        /*depends_on=*/ 3, // self-dependency via HEADERS
        /*weight=*/ 16,
        /*exclusive=*/ false,
        &[(":method", "GET"), (":path", "/")],
    );
    
    // Should be rejected
    let frame = server.receive_frame();
    assert!(matches!(frame, Some(Frame::RstStream(_)) | Some(Frame::GoAway(_))));
}
```

### Conformance Tests (tests/h2/priority_conformance.rs)

```rust
/// h2spec test: 5.3.1 - Stream Dependencies - A stream cannot depend on itself
#[test]
fn h2spec_5_3_1_self_dependency() {
    let mut conn = Connection::new_server();

            assert_eq!(error_code, ErrorCode::ProtocolError);
        }

    }
}

 {"id":"bd-2t4n","title":"Scheduler backoff: re-check queues after parking","description":"# Critical Bug: Backoff-to-Park Lost Wakeup Race

## Location
`src/runtime/scheduler/worker.rs:150-168`
`src/runtime/scheduler/three_lane.rs:228-264`

## Vulnerability Description

The backoff loop parks without re-checking the condition after waking:

```rust
loop {
    if !self.local.is_empty() || !self.global.is_empty() {
        break;
    }
    
    if backoff < SPIN_LIMIT {
        std::hint::spin_loop();
        backoff += 1;
    } else if backoff < SPIN_LIMIT + YIELD_LIMIT {
        std::thread::yield_now();
        backoff += 1;
    } else {
        self.parker.park();
        break;  // <-- BUG: No re-check after waking!
    }
}
```

## Race Condition

```
Worker:                        Task Injector:
───────                        ──────────────
is_empty() == true
backoff...
backoff...
                               inject(task)
                               unpark(worker)
parker.park()                  
park returns (woken)
break (exits loop)
continues to next phase
... never checks queue again
```

The worker was woken because work arrived, but it breaks out of the 
backoff loop without processing that work.

## Additional Issue in three_lane.rs

The three_lane version only checks global queue in backoff, not local:

```rust
loop {
    if !self.global.is_empty() {  // Only checks global!
        break;
    }
    // ...
}
```

This can cause unnecessary spinning when local queue has work.

## Impact

- **Severity**: CRITICAL (tasks left unprocessed)
- **Exploitability**: Timing-dependent, more likely under load
- **Impact**: Increased latency, potential task starvation

## Proposed Fix

After park() returns, re-check the condition instead of breaking:

```rust
loop {
    // Check all queues
    if !self.local.is_empty() || !self.global.is_empty() {
        break;
    }
    
    if backoff < SPIN_LIMIT {
        std::hint::spin_loop();
        backoff += 1;
    } else if backoff < SPIN_LIMIT + YIELD_LIMIT {
        std::thread::yield_now();
        backoff += 1;
    } else {
        self.parker.park();
        // Continue loop to re-check condition (no break!)
        backoff = 0;  // Reset backoff after waking
    }
}
```

## Why Reset Backoff?

After being woken, the worker should:
1. Re-check queues (handled by loop continuation)
2. If work found, process it
3. If no work (spurious wakeup), spin briefly before parking again

fn stress_backoff_rapid_inject_park_cycle() {
    init_test_logging();

#[test]
fn loom_backoff_inject_during_park() {
    loom::model(|| {
        let queue = Arc::new(Queue::new());
        let parker = Arc::new(Parker::new());
        let found_work = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
        
        // Worker thread: backoff and park
        let q1 = Arc::clone(&queue);
        let p1 = Arc::clone(&parker);
        let f1 = Arc::clone(&found_work);
        let worker = thread::spawn(move || {
            // Simulate backoff loop
            loop {
                if !q1.is_empty() {
                    f1.store(true, Ordering::Release);
                    break;
                }
                // Simulate reaching park point
                p1.park();
                // After wake: MUST re-check
                if !q1.is_empty() {
                    f1.store(true, Ordering::Release);
                    break;
                }
            }
        });
        
        // Injector thread: add work and unpark
        let q2 = Arc::clone(&queue);
        let p2 = Arc::clone(&parker);
        let injector = thread::spawn(move || {
            q2.push(1);
            p2.unpark();
        });
        
        worker.join().unwrap();
        injector.join().unwrap();
        
        // Work MUST be found
        assert!(found_work.load(Ordering::Acquire));
    });
}

        let queue = Arc::new(Queue::new());
        let parker = Arc::new(Parker::new());
        let iterations = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
        
        // Worker: may wake spuriously, should continue checking
        let q = Arc::clone(&queue);
        let p = Arc::clone(&parker);
        let i = Arc::clone(&iterations);
        let worker = thread::spawn(move || {
            loop {
                i.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                if !q.is_empty() {
                    break;
                }
                p.park();
                // Continue loop, don't break immediately
            }
        });
        
        // Inject after spurious wakes are possible
        thread::spawn(move || {
            queue.push(42);
            parker.unpark();
        });
        
        worker.join().unwrap();
        
        // Multiple iterations means spurious wakes handled
        // Work was eventually found
    });

 {"id":"bd-2tnl","title":"QUIC streams: bidirectional and unidirectional stream handling","description":"# QUIC Streams Implementation

## Overview
Implement QUIC stream handling with cancel-correct semantics for both
bidirectional and unidirectional streams.

## Design

### QuicConnection
```rust
pub struct QuicConnection {
    inner: quinn::Connection,
    streams: StreamTracker,
}

impl QuicConnection {
    /// Open bidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_bi(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        let (send, recv) = self.inner.open_bi().await?;
        self.streams.register_bi(send.id());
        
        Ok((
            SendStream::new(send, &self.streams),
            RecvStream::new(recv, &self.streams),
        ))
    }
    
    /// Open unidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_uni(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<SendStream, QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept bidirectional stream.
    pub async fn accept_bi(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept unidirectional stream.
    pub async fn accept_uni(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<RecvStream, QuicError>;
    
    /// Close connection gracefully.
    pub async fn close(&self, cx: &Cx, code: u32, reason: &[u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
}
```

### SendStream
```rust
pub struct SendStream {
    inner: quinn::SendStream,
    tracker: Arc<StreamTracker>,
}

impl SendStream {
    /// Write data to stream.
    pub async fn write(&mut self, cx: &Cx, data: &[u8]) -> Outcome<usize, QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        Ok(self.inner.write(data).await?)
    }
    
    /// Write all data.
    pub async fn write_all(&mut self, cx: &Cx, data: &[u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Finish sending (half-close).
    pub async fn finish(&mut self) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Reset stream with error code.
    pub fn reset(&mut self, code: u32);
}
```

### RecvStream
```rust
pub struct RecvStream {
    inner: quinn::RecvStream,
    tracker: Arc<StreamTracker>,
}

impl RecvStream {
    /// Read data from stream.
    pub async fn read(&mut self, cx: &Cx, buf: &mut [u8]) -> Outcome<Option<usize>, QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        Ok(self.inner.read(buf).await?)
    }
    
    /// Read exact amount.
    pub async fn read_exact(&mut self, cx: &Cx, buf: &mut [u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Read to end.
    pub async fn read_to_end(&mut self, cx: &Cx, limit: usize) -> Outcome<Vec<u8>, QuicError>;
    
    /// Stop reading with error code.
    pub fn stop(&mut self, code: u32);
}
```

## Cancellation Semantics
- SendStream reset with STOP_SENDING on cancel
- RecvStream stopped on cancel
- Connection tracks all streams for cleanup
- No data loss for already-transmitted frames
